nutrient_source.title: Pickled Pork Ears (Tai Heo Ngâm Chua Ngọt) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, record G00010586
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy (Primary regional source #1: HPB Singapore Foodo — Singapore dishes). Record cr_id G00010586, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are preserved verbatim from this canonical HPB record. HPB Singapore Foodo is an approved primary source for Singapore-served dishes including Vietnamese dishes sold in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Jenkins et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed); GI not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pickled pork ears contain 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Foodo record G00010586. Per established glycemic index methodology (Jenkins et al. 1981, Am J Clin Nutr), glycemic index is defined only for foods containing sufficient available carbohydrate to elicit a measurable blood glucose response; foods with negligible carbohydrate content are classified as not applicable. Glycemic index is therefore set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable'. Glycemic load per 90 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 0.0 by definition. This classification is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for protein- and fat-dominant foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1050 mg per 100 g, more than 5× the 200 mg per 100 g low-sodium threshold and well above the 600 mg per 100 g upper limit for hypertension-safe foods.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit pickled pork ears due to the very high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g); even a single 90 g serving delivers approximately 945 mg sodium, approaching half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content pose no direct glycemic load; however, very high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) may indirectly worsen insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk in people with Type 2 Diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pickled pork ears carry no glycemic burden due to negligible carbohydrates, but people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to minimise sodium-driven cardiovascular risk and potential worsening of insulin resistance; confirm brine recipe does not contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g) combined with significant cholesterol (89 mg/100 g) and total fat (10.5 g/100 g) from pork ear, a collagen-rich, fatty offal cut, presents a compounded cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should avoid pickled pork ears due to the combination of very high sodium, elevated cholesterol, and saturated fat from pork offal, all of which contribute to hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and atherosclerosis progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ear is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), a precursor to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) further exacerbates joint swelling in RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pickled pork ears as the high arachidonic acid content amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways and the very high sodium worsens tissue oedema around affected joints.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Pork Ears (Vietnamese: Tai Heo Ngâm Chua Ngọt) is a Vietnamese cold dish made from thinly sliced or whole pork ears that are simmered until tender and then submerged in a sweet-sour pickling brine typically composed of vinegar, sugar, salt, fish sauce, garlic, and chilli. The pork ear is prized for its distinctive dual texture — a firm, gelatinous outer layer of skin rich in collagen and a crunchy cartilaginous core — which creates a satisfying chew. The dish is served cold or at room temperature as an appetiser or side dish, and is commonly found in Vietnamese home cooking and Vietnamese-style eateries in Singapore. Nutritionally, pickled pork ears are high in protein (19.1 g/100 g) and moderate in fat (10.5 g/100 g), with negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g), making them glycemically inert. However, the sodium content is extremely high at 1050 mg per 100 g due to the salt, fish sauce, and brine used in pickling, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is notable at 89 mg/100 g. The dish provides iron (1.14 mg/100 g), calcium (19 mg/100 g), and magnesium (6 mg/100 g) from the bone-adjacent cartilage. It is not halal due to pork content and contains fish allergens from fish sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010586), an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, with a recorded confidence of 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. This is an exact match from a primary approved source.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010586); pork ear pickled in vinegar brine contains no starch or significant sugar in the meat itself — confirmed zero per HPB assay. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per the same HPB source; the pickling brine sugar is absorbed into the meat in negligible amounts per the HPB assay — confirmed zero. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as pork ear is an animal product containing no plant fibre. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pickled pork ears not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry G00010586 as of 2026-04-16; no supplementary approved source contains an exact match for this preparation. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this specific dish as of 2026-04-16; no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for Vietnamese pickled pork ears. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g per HPB); gi_source cites Jenkins et al. (1981) Am J Clin Nutr as the peer-reviewed methodological basis for the not-applicable classification, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology. This dish is not halal (contains pork). Fish sauce is a potential allergen. The input health_suitability field referenced 'rheumatoid_arthritis' which has been preserved in additional_conditions. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pig ear) is used as the primary ontology anchor. Nutrient source is HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary source (#1 in Project Veritas v2 source policy) for Singapore-served dishes.
